text: Leaving the playground is genuinely hard for toddlers — at 26 months, Mira doesn't yet have the cognitive tools to override a strong "I want to stay" feeling, so the meltdown is developmentally normal, not a behavior problem. The most evidence-backed move here is a **transition warning**: give her a short, consistent countdown before you leave ("five more minutes, then we go") and, per the **Responsive Parenting / Positive Discipline framework**, follow it with one small closing ritual she can count on — like letting her wave goodbye to the swings or pick which hand to hold on the way out — so her brain registers a predictable ending rather than an abrupt loss. That ritual works because toddlers regulate better when they can anticipate what comes next, not just hear "time to go." If you want to build on it, you could pair the warning with something to look forward to ("after the park we'll have your snack") — that's not a bribe, it's giving her a mental bridge to the next moment, which is a core strategy in the **Toddler Emotion Coaching framework**. Want me to save this approach as part of Mira's routine so I can remind you of it next time?
